content.text: Copyright Requires Human Authorship
Copyright law in all major jurisdictions requires that a work be the product of
human creative expression

. In the US, the Copyright Office has stated clearly (Copyright Registration Guidance, 2023) that it will not register works produced entirely by machine without creative input from a human author. The UK, EU, and most other jurisdictions take the same position. This means that the question of who
owns
an LLM output depends not on who clicked the button, but on how much original human creative expression is present in the output:
This sub-subtopic provides educational background. It is not legal advice. Consult qualified legal counsel for your specific situation and jurisdiction.
The Human Authorship Spectrum
The copyright status of AI-assisted work is not binary. It depends on how much original human creative expression — distinct from the AI's generative contribution — is present in the final work:

attrs.alt: Copyright protection spectrum from fully AI-generated (no copyright) to purely human-authored (full copyright), with intermediate cases

content.text: US Copyright Office Guidelines (2023)
The US Copyright Office published guidance in March 2023 addressing AI-generated works. The key principles are:
Purely AI-generated outputs are not copyrightable.
If a user provides a generic prompt and accepts the first output without modification, there is no human creative expression to protect.
The prompt itself may be copyrightable.

If the prompt involves substantial, original human creative expression — a detailed narrative, a specific poetic structure, a complex description — the prompt text is a copyrightable literary work. But the AI's output in response to it is not automatically protected.
Human selection and arrangement can be protected.

If a human selects, coordinates, and arranges AI-generated material in a way that requires original creative choices, that arrangement may be copyrightable — the way a photo collection can be copyrightable even if individual photos are not.
Disclosure is required.
Copyright applications must disclose which portions of a work were AI-generated. Failing to disclose voids the registration.
Platform Terms of Service as the Practical Framework
Because copyright protection for AI outputs is largely unavailable,
contractual rights granted by platform ToS

become the operative framework in practice. Most major LLM providers' terms grant the user ownership or a licence to use outputs, subject to the provider's own usage policies. These contractual rights are not the same as copyright and do not travel with the content — they bind the parties to the contract, not third parties:

from dataclasses import dataclass
from enum import Enum

class OutputRightType(str, Enum):
    COPYRIGHT        = "copyright"         # statutory copyright (rare for AI outputs)
    CONTRACTUAL      = "contractual"       # rights granted by platform ToS
    CREATIVE_COMMONS = "creative_commons"  # explicit CC licence on output
    PUBLIC_DOMAIN    = "public_domain"     # no protection (fully AI-generated)

@dataclass
class OutputRightsProfile:
    """
    Records the rights profile for an AI-generated or AI-assisted output.
    Used to determine what the user can do with the output commercially.
    """
    content_id: str
    platform: str                   # e.g. "OpenAI API", "Anthropic Claude API"
    human_contribution_estimate: float  # 0.0–1.0 (fraction of content that is human-authored)
    human_edits_documented: bool
    prompt_is_creative_work: bool   # if True, prompt itself may be copyrightable
    tos_grants_commercial_use: bool
    tos_url: str
    right_type: OutputRightType
    notes: str = ""

    @property
    def can_use_commercially(self) -> bool:
        return (
            self.right_type in (OutputRightType.COPYRIGHT, OutputRightType.CONTRACTUAL)
            and self.tos_grants_commercial_use
        )

    def copyright_recommendation(self) -> str:
        if self.human_contribution_estimate >= 0.7 and self.human_edits_documented:
            return "Register copyright; disclose AI use in application. Protect human-authored portions."
        elif self.human_contribution_estimate >= 0.3:
            return "Copyright registration possible for human-authored elements only. Consult legal counsel."
        else:
            return ("No copyright available for AI-generated portions. Rely on platform ToS for "
                    "commercial use rights. Consider Creative Commons zero (CC0) dedication for sharing.")


# Example profiles
openai_api_output = OutputRightsProfile(
    content_id="out_abc",
    platform="OpenAI API",
    human_contribution_estimate=0.05,   # generic prompt, unedited output
    human_edits_documented=False,
    prompt_is_creative_work=False,
    tos_grants_commercial_use=True,     # OpenAI grants commercial use via ToS
    tos_url="https://openai.com/policies/usage-policies",
    right_type=OutputRightType.CONTRACTUAL,
)
print(openai_api_output.copyright_recommendation())
# No copyright available for AI-generated portions. Rely on platform ToS...
What Can Be Protected: Worked Examples
Scenario
Human Contribution
US Copyright Status
Practical Approach
'Write me a blog post about coffee'
Near zero — generic prompt, unedited output
Not copyrightable
Rely on platform ToS for commercial use; consider CC0
Novelist uses AI to draft scenes, then heavily rewrites
High — substantial rewriting
Human-authored sections protected
Register copyright; disclose AI use; keep editing records
Designer prompts image AI with 100-word creative brief, curates 1/50 outputs
Medium — creative prompt + curation
Prompt may be protected; image itself contested
Register prompt as literary work; seek legal opinion on image
Marketing team generates 1,000 product descriptions via API
Template created by human; AI fills variables
Template is protected; filled outputs likely not
Assert copyright only on the template; use ToS rights for outputs
Researcher uses AI to translate their own paper to French
High in source; low in translation
Source protected; AI translation: unclear
Translator's creative choices in human-revised translation may qualify
Human Contribution Documentation

For work where copyright protection is sought, documenting the human contribution at creation time is essential — courts and copyright offices cannot verify the fraction of human authorship after the fact. The following schema captures the information needed for a copyright registration disclosure:

from dataclasses import dataclass, field
import hashlib

@dataclass
class HumanContributionRecord:
    """
    Proof-of-authorship record for AI-assisted creative work.
    Should be created at the time of authoring and stored immutably.
    """
    work_title: str
    creation_date: str            # ISO-8601
    author_name: str
    author_email: str

    prompt_text: str              # the exact prompt submitted to the AI
    ai_system: str                # e.g. "Claude 3.5 Sonnet (Anthropic)"
    ai_output_hash: str           # SHA-256 of the raw AI output
    final_work_hash: str          # SHA-256 of the final submitted work

    human_edits_description: str  # narrative description of what was changed
    human_contribution_estimate: float   # 0.0–1.0 (author's honest estimate)

    @property
    def edit_fraction(self) -> float:
        """Fraction of final work that differs from raw AI output."""
        return self.human_contribution_estimate

    def disclosure_statement(self) -> str:
        return (
            f"This work was created with AI assistance ({self.ai_system}). "
            f"The AI generated an initial draft in response to the prompt: "
            f"'{self.prompt_text[:100]}...'. "
            f"The human author ({self.author_name}) then revised the output substantially "
            f"({int(self.human_contribution_estimate * 100)} % of final content estimated as "
            f"human-authored). The raw AI output hash was {self.ai_output_hash[:16]}..."
        )


def hash_content(content: str) -> str:
    return hashlib.sha256(content.encode()).hexdigest()

# Usage:
# record = HumanContributionRecord(
#     work_title="Q3 Marketing Report",
#     creation_date="2025-01-15",
#     author_name="Jane Smith",
#     author_email="jane@example.com",
#     prompt_text="Draft a 500-word marketing report...",
#     ai_system="Claude 3.5 Sonnet",
#     ai_output_hash=hash_content(raw_ai_output),
#     final_work_hash=hash_content(final_text),
#     human_edits_description="Rewrote introduction, added three original case studies, corrected two factual errors.",
#     human_contribution_estimate=0.45,
# )
# print(record.disclosure_statement())
International Comparison
Jurisdiction
Position on AI Output Copyright
Key Reference
United States
No copyright without human authorship. AI outputs per se not protected. Human-authored portions of AI-assisted work may qualify.
Copyright Office Guidance, Feb 2023; Thaler v. Vidal, Fed. Cir. 2022
European Union
Similar to US — copyright requires human creative expression. EU does not extend copyright to AI per se.
Berne Convention Art. 2; CJEU case law requires personal intellectual creation
United Kingdom
Unique: CDPA s.9(3) grants copyright in computer-generated works to 'the person who undertakes the arrangements'. May cover AI output where a human arranged the generation.
Copyright, Designs and Patents Act 1988 s.9(3); consultation ongoing
China
Courts have found AI-generated works copyrightable where a human shows 'intellectual creation' in the prompt and selection (Beijing Internet Court, 2023).
Li v. Kuaishou Technology, Beijing Internet Court Nov 2023
Japan
Generally follows Berne — copyright requires human creative expression. AI-generated works not protected per se.
Japanese Copyright Act Art. 2(1)(i)
Practical Ownership Due Diligence
When deploying AI outputs commercially, a structured due-diligence checklist reduces exposure. The checklist below maps each risk category to a concrete mitigation step.
Risk Category
Key Question
Mitigation
Authorship chain
Was a human the proximate creative force?
Document every human decision: prompt engineering, selection, editing iterations
Training data
Does the model contain copyrighted expression that may bleed through?
Use models with auditable training data or indemnification clauses
Platform licence
Does the platform ToS grant you a suitable licence?
Read grant-back and sublicence clauses before commercial use
Jurisdiction
Where will the output be published / sold?
Identify destination country rules (US: human authorship; EU: software directive; CN: allows AI works with creativity test)
Registration
Is the human-contribution layer registrable?
Register the selection/arrangement with the copyright office where eligible
Run this checklist as part of each product launch involving generated content. Automate what you can — tool calls that record decisions are your audit trail.
Contractual Safeguards and Licensing Strategies

Even when copyright protection is uncertain, contracts fill the gap. Carefully drafted licence terms can give clients the certainty they need while accurately representing the ownership status of AI-generated material.
Vendor Indemnification

Major providers — OpenAI, Google, Microsoft, Amazon — now offer copyright indemnification programmes that cover litigation costs when outputs infringe third-party works, subject to conditions (no explicit infringement intent, use within permitted guidelines).
Always read the indemnification scope. Most exclude cases where you provided training data or fine-tuning material that is itself infringing.
Creative Commons and AI

If you distribute AI-generated content under a Creative Commons licence, the CC licence only grants what the licensor actually owns. If the output has no copyright (pure AI generation, no human selection), applying a CC licence is legally meaningless — you cannot licence what you do not own.
Best practice: apply CC licences only to the human-contributed elements (collection, curation, editorial additions) and state clearly what portions are AI-generated.
Work-for-Hire Clarity
For freelance and agency contracts, specify ownership of AI-assisted deliverables explicitly:

# Sample contract clause (simplified)
"Deliverables" means [project outputs]. Where Deliverables incorporate
AI-generated content, Contractor assigns to Client all rights Contractor
holds in the Deliverables, acknowledges that AI-generated portions may
not qualify for copyright protection in all jurisdictions, and warrants
that human creative contributions are original and not infringing.
This approach is honest about the uncertainty while still granting the broadest possible rights.
Provenance-Aware Output Pipeline
A production system that generates and publishes AI content should log enough metadata to reconstruct the ownership argument later. The following dataclass extends the
HumanContributionRecord
pattern into a full provenance record suitable for legal review.

from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from datetime import datetime, timezone
import hashlib, json

@dataclass
class OutputProvenanceRecord:
    output_id: str
    model_id: str                    # e.g. "gpt-4o-2024-11-20"
    model_provider: str              # e.g. "openai"
    platform_tos_version: str        # ToS version accepted by user
    prompt_author: str               # user / team identifier
    prompts: list[str]               # full prompt sequence
    human_edits: list[str]           # descriptions of manual edits
    selection_criteria: str          # how output was chosen from candidates
    final_text: str
    timestamp: str = field(default_factory=lambda: datetime.now(timezone.utc).isoformat())

    def human_contribution_score(self) -> float:
        """Rough heuristic: 0.0 (none) to 1.0 (fully human)."""
        score = 0.0
        if len(self.prompts) > 3:          score += 0.2   # iterative prompting
        if self.human_edits:               score += 0.4   # direct editing
        if self.selection_criteria:        score += 0.2   # curatorial choice
        if len(self.prompts[0]) > 300:     score += 0.2   # detailed creative direction
        return min(score, 1.0)

    def ownership_recommendation(self) -> str:
        score = self.human_contribution_score()
        if score >= 0.6:
            return "LIKELY_COPYRIGHTABLE: document human contributions for registration"
        elif score >= 0.3:
            return "BORDERLINE: seek legal review before asserting copyright"
        else:
            return "UNLIKELY_COPYRIGHTABLE: treat as public domain; use contracts"

    def fingerprint(self) -> str:
        payload = json.dumps({
            "output_id": self.output_id,
            "model_id": self.model_id,
            "prompts_hash": hashlib.sha256(str(self.prompts).encode()).hexdigest(),
            "final_text_hash": hashlib.sha256(self.final_text.encode()).hexdigest(),
        }, sort_keys=True)
        return hashlib.sha256(payload.encode()).hexdigest()

# Example
record = OutputProvenanceRecord(
    output_id="article-2025-06-30-001",
    model_id="gpt-4o-2024-11-20",
    model_provider="openai",
    platform_tos_version="2025-03-15",
    prompt_author="alice@example.com",
    prompts=[
        "Write a 600-word blog post about quantum computing for a general audience.",
        "Rewrite the second paragraph to use the analogy of a coin that is both heads and tails.",
        "Add a conclusion that connects to climate modelling applications.",
    ],
    human_edits=["Replaced opening sentence", "Added three statistics from our research"],
    selection_criteria="Chose draft 3 of 5 for its cleaner analogy and tighter conclusion",
    final_text="... (final published text) ...",
)

print(record.ownership_recommendation())
# LIKELY_COPYRIGHTABLE: document human contributions for registration
print(record.fingerprint()[:16])   # tamper-evident log key
Store one record per generated asset. The fingerprint lets you verify later that the provenance record has not been altered.
Summary

Copyright in AI-generated outputs depends on how much original human creative expression is present in the final work. In the US and most jurisdictions, purely AI-generated outputs are not copyrightable and fall into the public domain. Platform terms of service grant contractual usage rights that serve as the practical substitute for copyright in commercial contexts. Human-authored elements — a creative prompt, substantial rewrites, original selection and arrangement — can attract copyright protection if documented and disclosed to the copyright office. The spectrum runs from generic-prompt outputs (public domain) through heavily edited AI drafts (partially protected) to AI-as-tool works (full human copyright). As law evolves, documentation of human contribution at creation time is the most important protection practitioners can implement today.

You can build a business on AI-generated content without copyright — but you cannot stop a competitor from using the same AI to produce the same output. Copyright protection requires genuine human creative choices, and those choices must be documented at the time they are made.
